content_data.tip1.headline: Book Your Trial 3 Months Out
content_data.tip1.detail: Most wedding photographers are booked solid 4-6 months before peak season. Lock the trial first, then negotiate the package.
content_data.tip2.headline: Send a Shot List, Not a Pinterest Board
content_data.tip2.detail: A 200-pin Pinterest board is a vibe, not a brief. Pick 12 photos that capture moments you want — your photographer will do the rest.
content_data.tip3.headline: Don't Skip the Recce
content_data.tip3.detail: A venue scout an hour before sunset tells your photographer where the light falls. Skip it, and you're shooting blind during the muhurat.
content_data.tip3.annotation: one hour can save the album
content_data.tip4.headline: Feed Your Vendors
content_data.tip4.detail: A 14-hour shoot day on no food shows up in the photos. Plan a vendor meal and a 20-minute break — your album will thank you.

content_data.items:
Confirm capacity for your actual guest count
Walk the baraat path — is it photogenic?
Ask about sound restrictions and curfew
Check power load for DJ + lights + caterer
Locate the bridal suite and groom prep room
Time the route from parking to the mandap
Ask about backup options for rain / heat
Request the in-house decor list and exclusions
Get the catering kitchen tour (smell test included)
Clarify what's locked in writing vs. 'we usually do'

content_data.styleSubtitle: for the bride who wants sunlight and petals
content_data.slides.0.title: THE VIBE
content_data.slides.0.body: Open lawns, dappled sunlight, and the soft sound of a tabla drifting from somewhere behind the hedges. The garden wedding is a long, slow afternoon you'll wish lasted longer.
content_data.slides.1.title: BEST MONTHS
content_data.slides.1.body: October through February. Avoid the monsoon — petrichor is romantic until it's a soaked mandap. March can work in higher altitudes, but heatstroke is not a vibe.
content_data.slides.2.title: WATCH OUT FOR
content_data.slides.2.body: Mosquitoes at dusk, sound permits in residential pockets, and the wind picking up after sunset. Plan for citronella, a backup mic, and a wrap for grandma.

content_data.tips.0.headline: Lead with the package
content_data.tips.0.detail: Don't ask for the price. Ask what's included in the standard package, then negotiate items in or out. The price reveals itself.
content_data.tips.1.headline: Bundle the events
content_data.tips.1.detail: Mehndi + Sangeet + Wedding + Reception is four events to one vendor. The per-event rate drops 20-30% when bundled. Always ask.
content_data.tips.2.headline: Get three quotes
content_data.tips.2.detail: Three written quotes for the same scope. Share the lowest with the others — politely. Real vendors will match or explain why their price is higher.
content_data.tips.3.headline: Lock the deliverables
content_data.tips.3.detail: Get every deliverable on paper — counts, formats, deadlines. 'Edited photos' isn't a deliverable. '500 edited photos in 6 weeks' is.
content_data.bottomLine: Confidence is free. Bad contracts aren't.

content_data.regionSubtitle: energy, emotion, and a LOT of dancing
content_data.slides.0.title: Roka, Sangeet, Phere — back to back to back
content_data.slides.0.content: A Punjabi wedding stretches across at least four events: Roka, Sangeet, Mehendi, and the wedding day itself with Anand Karaj or pheras. Expect a baraat with a horse, dhol players, and a procession that takes its time.
content_data.slides.1.title: Lehengas, paranda, and a sherwani for him
content_data.slides.1.content: Brides typically wear a heavily embroidered red or pink lehenga with kundan jewelry, a kaleera tied to the wrist, and a paranda braided into the hair. The groom wears a sherwani with a sehra or safa turban.
content_data.slides.2.title: Sarson da saag, butter chicken, and the chaat counter
content_data.slides.2.content: Punjabi weddings live and die by the food. The buffet is non-negotiable: tandoori spreads, paneer in three forms, kulfi falooda, and a live chaat counter that runs all night. Dessert is jalebi with rabri, always.
content_data.slides.3.title: Dhol, bhangra, and the DJ that knows every Diljit drop
content_data.slides.3.content: Live dhol players lead the baraat. The sangeet is choreographed by the cousins. Expect bhangra, gidda, and a DJ who can flip from old-school Daler Mehndi to Diljit without losing the dance floor for a single beat.
content_data.slides.4.title: Kaleere, joota chupai, and the chura ceremony
content_data.slides.4.content: The chura ceremony — red and white bangles slipped on by the bride's mama — is signature Punjabi. Joota chupai (stealing the groom's shoes) is a friendly ransom war. And kaleere are dropped on cousins to bless the next bride.

content_data.roles.0.title: The Mama
content_data.roles.0.description: Mom's brother. Will give a speech at the sangeet whether anyone asked or not.
content_data.roles.1.title: The Bua
content_data.roles.1.description: Dad's sister. Has very specific opinions about the menu, the flowers, and the cousin's outfit.
content_data.roles.2.title: The Chacha
content_data.roles.2.description: Dad's younger brother. The cool uncle. Will sneak you the good whisky at the cocktail hour.
content_data.roles.3.title: The Nani
content_data.roles.3.description: Mom's mom. The matriarch. Will cry the moment you walk down the aisle. Bring a tissue.
content_data.roles.4.title: The Bhabhi
content_data.roles.4.description: Your secret co-conspirator. Knows everything about everyone and shares strategically.

content_data.tip1.headline: Book Your Trial 3 Months Out
content_data.tip1.detail: Most wedding photographers are booked solid 4-6 months before peak season. Lock the trial first, then negotiate the package.
content_data.tip2.headline: Send a Shot List, Not a Pinterest Board
content_data.tip2.detail: A 200-pin Pinterest board is a vibe, not a brief. Pick 12 photos that capture moments you want — your photographer will do the rest.
content_data.tip3.headline: Don't Skip the Recce
content_data.tip3.detail: A venue scout an hour before sunset tells your photographer where the light falls. Skip it, and you're shooting blind during the muhurat.
content_data.tip3.annotation: one hour can save the album
content_data.tip4.headline: Feed Your Vendors
content_data.tip4.detail: A 14-hour shoot day on no food shows up in the photos. Plan a vendor meal and a 20-minute break — your album will thank you.
